title: The Expanding Path
concept: The expanding path (decoder) of U-Net uses transposed convolutions and regular convolutions to restore spatial resolution and project features back to pixel space.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main structural trend as features move down the contracting path?

options.text: Spatial resolution decreases while channel depth increases
Spatial resolution increases while channel depth decreases
Both spatial resolution and channel depth decrease

exercises.explain: Correct! The contracting path shrinks space and grows channels.

cards.title: Climbing back up
cards.body: Now that we've reached the bottom of the 'U' (the bottleneck), we have a compact, 512-channel map full of abstract ideas. Now, we must climb the right side: the expanding path. How do we turn these abstract ideas back into a pixel-precise mask?

cards.title: The Expanding Path
cards.body: The Expanding Path (or Decoder) is the right side of the U-Net. It uses transposed convolutions to double the spatial resolution and halve the channels at each step, followed by regular 3x3 convolutions to refine the upsampled features.

cards.title: Blowing up a balloon
cards.body: Imagine the expanding path as carefully inflating a collapsed, decorated balloon. As it grows, the details stretch back out, and we use local brushes (convolutions) to paint the sharp borders back in.

cards.title: Channel halving
cards.body: Starting at the bottleneck with 512 channels, the expanding path upsamples to 256 channels, then to 128, then to 64, and finally uses a 1x1 convolution to output a mask with classes matching the target.

cards.title: No dense layers in the decoder
cards.body: You might think the decoder needs dense layers to make the final class decision. It doesn't! The final layer of the expanding path is a simple 1x1 convolution that maps each pixel's channels to the class probabilities.

cards.title: Expanding Path Layout
cards.body: How the right side of the U-Net works:

right.text:
Resolution: 256x256, Channels: Class Count
Pixel-level segmentation mask

exercises.prompt: What layer does the expanding path use to double the spatial resolution?
exercises.explain: Correct! Transposed convolutions are used for learnable upsampling in the decoder.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The final layer of U-Net is a 1x1 convolution that maps features to the target class count.
exercises.explain: True. The final 1x1 convolution produces the class probability map for every pixel.

exercises.sentence: The expanding path halves the channel depth and ___ the spatial resolution at each block.
exercises.explain: The expanding path restores spatial dimensions by doubling the resolution.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of the expanding path?

options.text: To restore spatial resolution and localize features precisely
To compress feature maps to save memory

exercises.explain: It maps abstract features back to pixel coordinates to create the mask.

exercises.prompt: Why does the expanding path have convolutions after every transposed convolution upsampling step?

options.text: To help the network learn how to smooth out upsampling artifacts and blend the merged features.
To reduce the spatial resolution back to the bottleneck size.
To classify the overall category of the image.
To freeze the weights so they cannot be trained.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Upsampling alone can create rough or blocky features; subsequent convolutions help smooth and refine the mask. Next: how do we connect the left and right sides of the U?
